title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 04

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which World War I changed relationships between imperial states and colonial peoples from 1914 to 1930.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Letter from an Indian soldier serving with British forces in France to his brother in Punjab, 1915.
Source Type: Personal letter.
“We have crossed seas I never imagined and now fight in cold mud for the King-Emperor. The officers praise our courage, and men from many lands stand beside us. Yet I wonder whether loyalty shown in battle will be remembered when the war ends. If India gives blood and money, should India not also receive honor and a greater voice in her own affairs?”
Sourcing cues: Colonial soldier writing privately during wartime; expresses loyalty and expectation of political recognition.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Circular from a French colonial governor to district officers in West Africa, 1916.
Source Type: Administrative circular.
“The Republic requires additional men for labor battalions, transport services, and military support. Chiefs should be reminded that loyal cooperation will bring favor after victory. Recruitment must be energetic but orderly, and reports should emphasize the honor of defending civilization against German aggression.”
Sourcing cues: Colonial administrative perspective; reveals wartime demands on colonial subjects and official language of loyalty.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Wartime service summary compiled by an imperial statistics office, 1919.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Colonial and Overseas Contributions during World War I
Region / Population Group Type of Service Described
British India Combat troops, laborers, money, supplies
French West Africa Soldiers, porters, agricultural production
North Africa Soldiers and laborers in European theaters
China Labor corps for transport, repair, and logistics
Caribbean colonies Volunteers, funds, and shipping labor
Pacific island communities Labor, local defense, and resource supply
Note on the table: The figures were presented by imperial governments as evidence of empire-wide loyalty and shared sacrifice.
Sourcing cues: Official statistical framing; highlights contributions but may understate coercion, casualties, or dissent.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a British recruitment poster distributed in northern India, 1917.
Source Type: Described poster.
The poster shows a uniformed Indian soldier standing beside a British officer under a banner reading “Serve with Honor.” Behind them are images of the Union Jack, a village elder blessing departing recruits, and a battlefield where Indian cavalry charge forward. At the bottom, the text promises pay, family allowances, and respect for loyal service.
Sourcing cues: Recruitment propaganda; designed to persuade colonial subjects by appealing to honor, loyalty, masculinity, and material benefits.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Petition from Egyptian nationalist leaders to British officials, 1919, Cairo.
Source Type: Political petition.
“During the war, Egypt’s fields, railways, animals, and laborers were placed at the disposal of the British army. We were told that the struggle was for liberty and the rights of nations. We therefore request recognition of Egypt’s right to send representatives abroad and to determine its own political future without military occupation.”
Sourcing cues: Nationalist elite petition after wartime mobilization; uses wartime rhetoric of self-determination against imperial control.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Declaration by Korean students after demonstrations against Japanese rule, 1919, Seoul.
Source Type: Student declaration.
“The world speaks now of justice among nations. We do not seek revenge against the Japanese people, but we reject rule that denies our language, associations, and dignity. If small nations in Europe may claim self-determination, Korea must not be treated as an exception in Asia.”
Sourcing cues: Anti-colonial student perspective; invokes global postwar language to challenge Japanese imperial rule.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Memoir excerpt by a Senegalese veteran of French service, written in 1928, Dakar.
Source Type: Memoir excerpt.
“In France, we learned that the empire needed us when danger came. Some officers spoke to us as comrades; others treated us as children. When we returned, medals did not protect us from taxes or insults. Still, the journey changed many of us. We had seen the weakness of Europeans and heard new words about rights.”
Sourcing cues: Veteran’s retrospective account; emphasizes changed expectations and contradictions of imperial service.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which World War I changed relationships between imperial states and colonial peoples from 1914 to 1930. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that the war intensified imperial demands while encouraging colonial subjects to make stronger claims for rights, representation, and self-determination.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to imperialism and World War I, such as nineteenth-century imperial expansion, colonial labor and taxation, European rivalries, wartime mobilization, Wilsonian self-determination, the Russian Revolution, the League of Nations, or postwar nationalist movements.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about wartime mobilization, colonial military service, propaganda, labor demands, postwar nationalist claims, self-determination, or changing perceptions of European authority.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the Amritsar massacre, the May Fourth Movement, the Wafd Party, the mandate system, the Balfour Declaration, Pan-African Congresses, Ho Chi Minh’s appeal at Versailles, the Rowlatt Acts, or wartime African troop recruitment.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why recruitment posters appealed to honor and benefits, why imperial statistics emphasized loyalty, why nationalist petitions used wartime language of liberty, or why veterans’ memories highlighted contradictions in imperial rule.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of imperial relationships after World War I. This may include explaining both continuity and change, showing that imperial rule often persisted even as colonial expectations changed, comparing different empires, or analyzing how wartime loyalty could become a basis for anti-imperial claims.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that World War I did not immediately end imperial rule, but it changed relationships between imperial states and colonial peoples by increasing colonial military and labor demands while giving colonized groups new arguments for rights and self-determination. Documents 1, 2, 3, and 4 can support arguments about imperial mobilization and wartime loyalty. Documents 5, 6, and 7 can support arguments about postwar nationalism, disappointment, and changed perceptions of empire. Outside evidence could include Amritsar, May Fourth, the Wafd Party, the mandate system, Ho Chi Minh at Versailles, or Pan-African Congresses. Strong sourcing might analyze propaganda purpose, official statistical framing, nationalist use of wartime rhetoric, and veterans’ retrospective perspective.
